era_context: The Second Punic War (218–201 BCE) is in its middle stages, with Rome and Carthage locked in a prolonged struggle for dominance in the Mediterranean. Hannibal is conducting his famous campaign in Italy, while Rome is preparing for a counter-offensive. In the east, the Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III is expanding, and the Ptolemaic Empire is weakening. The Han Dynasty in China is consolidating power after the civil wars of the Chu-Han period. This is a year of military stalemate and strategic preparation.

events.title: Scipio Africanus appointed to command in Sicily and North Africa
events.description: Scipio Africanus, a young and ambitious Roman general, was appointed to command Roman forces in Sicily and to prepare for an invasion of North Africa. This appointment was controversial, as Scipio was relatively young and had limited military experience. However, his appointment marked a turning point in Roman strategy in the Second Punic War. Scipio's plan was to invade North Africa and force Hannibal to abandon his campaign in Italy to defend Carthage. This strategy would ultimately prove successful, leading to the Battle of Zama in 202 BCE and Roman victory.
events.certainty_note: Multiple near-contemporary sources confirm Scipio's appointment in 204 BCE. Livy and Polybius wrote within ~150 years. The date is secure within ±1 year.
cross_references: Led to -203-001 (Scipio's invasion of North Africa)

events.title: Hannibal continues campaign in southern Italy
events.description: Hannibal continued his military campaign in southern Italy, maintaining his presence on the Italian peninsula despite Roman efforts to contain him. By 204 BCE, Hannibal had been campaigning in Italy for about 14 years without achieving a decisive victory. His inability to capture Rome or break Roman resolve was becoming increasingly apparent. However, Hannibal remained a formidable military force and continued to pose a threat to Roman interests. His campaign in Italy is considered one of the most impressive military achievements of the ancient world, despite its ultimate failure.
events.certainty_note: Hannibal's presence in Italy in 204 BCE is well-attested, but no specific event is recorded for this year. The certainty is 'probable' based on the broader context of his campaign.
cross_references: Part of broader Hannibal's Italian campaign (-218 to -203 BCE)

events.title: Antiochus III's eastern campaigns; expansion of Seleucid power
events.description: Antiochus III of the Seleucid Empire was conducting military campaigns in the east, expanding Seleucid control over territories in Central Asia and the Iranian plateau. These eastern campaigns were part of Antiochus's broader strategy to restore the Seleucid Empire to its former glory. The campaigns were successful in recovering territories that had broken away from Seleucid control, and they demonstrated Antiochus's military prowess. However, the eastern campaigns also distracted Antiochus from the west, where the Ptolemaic Empire was weakening and Rome was rising.
events.certainty_note: Antiochus III's eastern campaigns are well-attested, but the specific dating of individual campaigns is approximate. The year 204 BCE falls within his period of eastern expansion, but no source explicitly dates a specific event to 204 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader Antiochus III's expansion (-212 to -188 BCE)

events.title: Han Dynasty: Kangxi's reign; consolidation of power
events.description: Emperor Kangxi of the Han Dynasty (r. 157–141 BCE) continued his reign during this period. The early Han Dynasty was consolidating power after the civil wars of the Chu-Han period. The year 204 BCE falls within the early consolidation phase of the Han Dynasty, a period of relative stability and prosperity. Kangxi's reign was marked by the continuation of the heqin policy with the Xiongnu and the gradual expansion of Han influence.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, Chapters 10–12
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu, Chapters 1–4

events.certainty_note: Kangxi's reign is well-attested, but the year 204 BCE does not correspond to any specific recorded event. The certainty is 'probable' based on the broader context of his reign.
cross_references: Part of broader Han Dynasty consolidation period (-206 to -141 BCE)

events.title: Ptolemy IV Philopator continues reign; Egypt weakens
events.description: Ptolemy IV Philopator (r. 221–202 BCE) continued his reign over Egypt in 204 BCE. His reign was marked by internal instability, military weakness, and the gradual loss of Egyptian influence in the eastern Mediterranean. The Fourth Syrian War (219–217 BCE) had demonstrated Egypt's military weakness, and Ptolemy IV's reign saw the beginning of the decline of Ptolemaic power. His death in 202 BCE would be followed by the succession of his young son, Ptolemy V, which would further weaken Egypt and invite intervention from neighboring powers.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy IV's reign is well-attested, but the year 204 BCE does not correspond to any specific recorded event. The certainty is 'probable' based on the broader context of his reign.
cross_references: Led to -202-004 (Death of Ptolemy IV and succession of Ptolemy V)

disconfirming_evidence: Some modern scholars debate the exact date of Scipio's appointment to command in Sicily and North Africa, with some sources placing it in 205 BCE rather than 204 BCE. However, the majority of scholars place it in 204 BCE. The significance of Scipio's appointment is not disputed. Additionally, some scholars argue that Scipio's strategy of invading North Africa was not as innovative as traditionally portrayed; earlier Roman commanders had considered similar strategies.
historiographic_note: The year 204 BCE is well-documented for the Mediterranean world through Greek and Roman sources (Livy, Polybius, Appian). The Second Punic War is one of the most extensively documented conflicts in ancient history. Chinese sources provide context for the Han Dynasty but are less detailed for this specific year. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic and social history are underrepresented. The sources reflect Roman and Greek perspectives; Carthaginian sources are almost entirely lost.

graph_edges.note: Scipio's appointment to command led to his invasion of North Africa
Scipio's appointment to command ultimately led to the Battle of Zama
Ptolemy IV's continued weakness led to his death and the succession of Ptolemy V
